title: Object records and property access

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Object records and property access; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Object records and property access
sources.claim: Objects associate property keys with values through own and inherited properties, descriptors, and prototype lookup.

sources.locator: MDN JavaScript Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Object records and property access
sources.claim: Objects associate property keys with values through own and inherited properties, descriptors, and prototype lookup.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Objects associate property keys with values through own and inherited properties, descriptors, and prototype lookup.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Object.hasOwn distinguishes an own configuration key from one inherited through the prototype chain. Your goal is to model and validate a small domain record with property shorthand and safe access. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Control flow and functions; pf data selection; let, const and binding; pf data records. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Objects associate property keys with values through own and inherited properties, descriptors, and prototype lookup. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Enumeration order has rules, symbols are separate keys, spreading is shallow, and untrusted keys can affect prototypes. This draft therefore rejects: The in operator checks only an object's own properties.

includes: Create record-like objects, use computed properties and distinguish missing from undefined values.
Model and validate a small domain record with property shorthand and safe access.
excludes: Enumeration order has rules, symbols are separate keys, spreading is shallow, and untrusted keys can affect prototypes.

outcomes: Model and validate a small domain record with property shorthand and safe access.

examples.title: Object records and property access: concrete case
examples.situation: Object.hasOwn distinguishes an own configuration key from one inherited through the prototype chain.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Object.hasOwn distinguishes an own configuration key from one inherited through the prototype chain.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: The in operator includes inherited properties; use Object.hasOwn when only direct ownership is valid.
examples.result: The result follows because The in operator includes inherited properties; use Object.hasOwn when only direct ownership is valid.

examples.title: Object records and property access: boundary case

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: The in operator checks only an object's own properties.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: The in operator includes inherited properties; use Object.hasOwn when only direct ownership is valid.
